backup_api_paths: /metrics (port 8085, no auth by default) — NOT a REST list API; the velero_backup_* gauges and the schedule/backupName/backup_location_name labels enumerate every backup and schedule name plus counts/last-status/last-successful-timestamp. This is the only unauth HTTP listing surface Velero core itself serves.
/debug/pprof/ (port 6060, only when --profiler-address is enabled) — Go pprof; leaks goroutines/heap incl. in-flight bucket paths and credentials env, not a backup list but a metadata-leak sibling.

OBJECT-STORE (the real list surface, not a Velero port): GET https://<bucket>.s3.<region>.amazonaws.com/?prefix=backups/ (or Azure/GCS equivalent) on a world-readable BackupStorageLocation bucket — Velero itself lists backups by enumerating folders under the /backups/ sub-path; an open bucket reproduces that listing for an anonymous reader.

THIRD-PARTY UI (separate fingerprint, not Velero core): otwld velero-ui and seriohub VUI expose HTTP/WebSocket backup-listing APIs (e.g. velero-ui /api/v1/backups) that, if deployed without their auth layer, list backups over HTTP.
ai_scope_discriminator: Velero snapshots whole namespaces, so the in-scope gate is "does the backup contain an AI/ML namespace or workload," confirmed from NAMES/MANIFEST only: (1) In the open object bucket, list keys under backups/<name>/ and read velero-backup.json — its spec.includedNamespaces / status fields and the resource-list.json.gz name the namespaces and CRD kinds captured. AI/ML in-scope if you see namespaces like kubeflow / mlflow / kserve / ray / seldon / triton, or backed-up CRD kinds notebooks.kubeflow.org, inferenceservices.serving.kserve.io, rayclusters.ray.io, or PVC/PV names for model-artifact or vector-store volumes (qdrant-, milvus-, weaviate-, chromadb-, pgvector). (2) For PV data, the restic/ prefix or kopia/<namespace>/ path itself names the namespace — kopia/kubeflow or kopia/mlflow is the discriminator without reading a single blob. Generic substrate (a plain Postgres/etcd-only backup, system namespaces only) is OUT of scope. Reading the velero-backup.json manifest and the kopia/restic prefix names is metadata; downloading contents.tar.gz or the kopia repo blobs is exfil and forbidden.
fingerprint_markers: # HELP velero_backup_total Current number of existent backups
podVolume_ prefix on node-agent /metrics (restic/kopia file-system backup)
fp_guards: Any Prometheus /metrics endpoint exposes go_* and process_* counters — those do NOT confirm Velero. Require at least one velero_-prefixed series (velero_backup_total or a velero_backup_*{schedule=} label) before tagging the host Velero.

kube-state-metrics can surface kube_customresource_* series derived from Velero Backup CRDs that contain the word 'velero' — exclude by requiring the literal velero_backup_total HELP line / native velero_ namespace, not a kube_ wrapper.

Broad http.title:Velero matches the third-party seriohub/otwld dashboards and blog/marketing pages, NOT a Velero server — those have no /metrics velero_ series; gate on the metrics body, not the title.

An open S3/Azure/GCS bucket with a backups/ key prefix is not necessarily Velero (any tool can name a folder 'backups') — confirm by the presence of a velero-backup.json object inside backups/<name>/ before calling it Velero.
8085 is also a common generic app/dev port (Spring Boot, misc) — a 200 on 8085 alone is nothing; only the velero_ metric strings confirm.

cves.desc: Velero restore package improperly manages volume identifiers, causing information leakage to unauthorized users; fixed in 1.4.3 / 1.5.2.
Vulnerability affecting velero and velero-restore-helper (restore-time handling); fixed in 1.17.1-r2 image build — review the restore path before trusting a restore-helper-enabled deployment.
golang.org/x/net (vendored into velero-plugin-for-aws) non-linear parse complexity DoS; transitive image CVE, not a Velero-logic flaw, but present in older plugin images.
go/build/constraint stack-exhaustion in velero-plugin-for-csi image (transitive Go stdlib); image-level, not endpoint RCE.

pivot_paths: 1. Confirm Velero: curl http://<host>:8085/metrics | grep '^velero_backup' — read the velero_backup_total gauge (count of existing backups) and harvest backup/schedule names from {schedule=...} and {backupName=...} labels. NAMES ONLY.

2. Read posture, not data: velero_backup_last_status (1/0) and velero_backup_last_successful_timestamp{schedule=...} reveal which schedules exist and their last success time — operator/freshness profiling without touching the store.

3. Pivot to the snapshot store ONLY if the BackupStorageLocation bucket is independently world-readable: list keys under backups/ (S3 ?prefix=backups/ or az/gsutil ls) to enumerate backup folder names — this mirrors Velero's own folder-listing discovery and stays at the metadata layer.

4. For each backup name of interest, read the small metadata object backups/<name>/velero-backup.json and resource-list.json.gz to learn includedNamespaces and captured CRD kinds — apply the AI-scope discriminator here.
5. Read prefix names only: list (do not fetch) restic/ and kopia/<namespace>/ to confirm which namespaces have PV-level data captured. The namespace appears in the path itself.
6. STOP. The next object (contents.tar.gz, the kopia repo blobs, any *.tar.gz) is the actual cluster snapshot — that is exfil, out of bounds.
restraint_note: Names and manifests are the finding. Velero core serves no auth-bypassable backup-download endpoint; the /metrics labels and the velero-backup.json/resource-list manifests are metadata and may be read to confirm severity and AI-scope. Downloading any backup tarball (contents.tar.gz), reading restic/kopia repository blobs, or fetching a PV snapshot = data exfiltration of a whole-cluster image including secrets — forbidden. POSTing a Velero Restore CR, hitting a third-party UI restore action, or any write to the bucket = a write/destructive primitive — forbidden. Enumerate to the manifest, score from the names, never pull the snapshot.
